startScreenOptions.title: BI2.1-BI2.5 — Interactive Lecture (BI)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 6 embedded questions for BI2.1-BI2.5.

interactions.label: Which property of enzymes explains why sucrase breaks down sucrose but will not

params.question: Which property of enzymes explains why sucrase breaks down sucrose but will not act on lactose?

answers.text: Efficiency — it accelerates reactions by factors of millions

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Efficiency refers to the rate of catalysis, not substrate selectivity. Enzymes achieve selectivity through the precise shape of their active site, which is a different property.

answers.text: Specificity — each enzyme works on one particular substrate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Specificity means each enzyme recognises one particular substrate. Sucrase's active site is shaped to bind sucrose but not lactose — this selectivity comes from the complementary shape of the active site pocket.

answers.text: Regulation — the cell can adjust enzyme activity as needed

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Regulation refers to the cell's ability to increase or decrease enzyme activity to meet current metabolic needs, not the enzyme's substrate selectivity.

answers.text: Mild conditions — enzymes work at body temperature and physiological pH

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Mild conditions describe the temperature and pH range at which enzymes operate. The ability to distinguish one substrate from another is governed by specificity, not operating conditions.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the four defining properties: specificity (substrate selectivity via active-site shape), efficiency (rate acceleration by lowering activation energy), regulation (cellular control of activity), and mild conditions (body temperature, physiological pH).
Specificity is what makes enzymes selective. The shape of the active site determines which substrate can bind — sucrase only accommodates sucrose, not lactose or other disaccharides.

interactions.label: In a patient with suspected myocardial infarction, which isoenzyme finding in th

params.question: In a patient with suspected myocardial infarction, which isoenzyme finding in the blood would support the diagnosis?

answers.text: Elevated LDH5 with normal LDH1

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: LDH5, composed of four M subunits, predominates in the liver and skeletal muscle. Its elevation points to liver disease, not myocardial infarction.

answers.text: LDH1 level rising above LDH2

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Normally LDH2 exceeds LDH1 in blood. When LDH1 (four H subunits, predominant in the heart) rises above LDH2, this 'flipped' pattern is characteristic of myocardial infarction.

answers.text: Elevated CK-BB with normal CK-MB

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: CK-BB originates in the brain and is rarely detectable in blood. CK-MB, not CK-BB, is the cardiac-specific creatine kinase isoform used to diagnose myocardial infarction.

answers.text: Elevated CK-MM with normal CK-MB

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: CK-MM predominates in skeletal muscle. Its elevation suggests skeletal muscle injury, not cardiac damage. CK-MB is the isoform that signals myocardial damage.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: LDH has five isoforms built from H and M subunits. LDH1 (four H subunits) predominates in the heart. Normally LDH2 > LDH1, so a reversal (the 'flip') indicates heart damage. For CK, the cardiac-specific isoform is CK-MB.
The LDH flip — LDH1 exceeding LDH2 — is a classic marker of heart muscle damage, because LDH1 (four H subunits) predominates in the heart.

interactions.label: In the induced fit model, the enzyme's active site is rigid and only allows the

params.question: In the induced fit model, the enzyme's active site is rigid and only allows the correctly shaped substrate to bind.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This statement actually describes the lock-and-key model, not the induced fit model. In induced fit, the active site is flexible — it changes shape when the substrate approaches, like a hand closing around a ball. Because proteins are flexible rather than rigid, the induced fit model is more accurate.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. A rigid active site describes the older lock-and-key model (Fischer, 1894). In the induced fit model (Koshland, 1958), the active site is flexible and changes shape when the substrate arrives — wrapping around it to position catalytic residues, strain the substrate, and exclude water.

interactions.label: An enzyme has a low Km for its substrate. What does this indicate?

params.question: An enzyme has a low Km for its substrate. What does this indicate?

answers.text: The enzyme has a low maximum reaction rate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Km and Vmax are independent parameters. A low Km says nothing about the maximum rate. Vmax depends on enzyme concentration and catalytic efficiency, not on substrate affinity.

answers.text: The enzyme needs a high substrate concentration to reach half of Vmax

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes a high Km. A high-Km enzyme needs abundant substrate to reach half its maximum rate, indicating loose binding and low affinity for its substrate.

answers.text: The enzyme has high affinity for its substrate and reaches half-Vmax at low substrate concentrations

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Low Km means the enzyme reaches half of Vmax at a low substrate concentration — like a taxi that fills up quickly even on a quiet street. This indicates tight binding and high affinity.

answers.text: The enzyme is saturated and cannot bind any more substrate

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Saturation occurs when all active sites are occupied, which is the state at Vmax. Km describes the substrate concentration at which the rate is half of Vmax, not a saturation state.

overallFeedback.feedback: Km is the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is exactly half of Vmax. Low Km = tight binding (high affinity); high Km = loose binding (low affinity). Think of the taxi analogy: a low-Km taxi fills up quickly even on a quiet street.
Low Km = high affinity. The enzyme reaches half its maximum speed at a low substrate concentration, meaning it binds substrate tightly and efficiently.

metadata.title: An enzyme has a low Km for its substrate

interactions.label: L-asparaginase is used to treat acute lymphoblastic leukaemia because:

params.question: L-asparaginase is used to treat acute lymphoblastic leukaemia because:

answers.text: It directly destroys leukaemic cell membranes

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: L-asparaginase does not directly attack cancer cells. It works indirectly by depleting an essential amino acid from the bloodstream that certain cancer cells cannot synthesise on their own.

answers.text: It activates plasminogen to dissolve clots surrounding tumour cells

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Activating plasminogen to dissolve fibrin clots is the function of streptokinase and tissue plasminogen activator (tPA), used in myocardial infarction and stroke — not in leukaemia treatment.

answers.text: It depletes blood asparagine, starving cancer cells that cannot synthesise their own

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Certain ALL cells lack the ability to synthesise asparagine and depend entirely on the blood supply. L-asparaginase removes circulating asparagine, selectively starving these cancer cells.

answers.text: It replaces a deficient enzyme within the leukaemic cells

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Enzyme replacement therapy (e.g., imiglucerase for Gaucher disease, agalsidase for Fabry disease) replaces missing enzymes. L-asparaginase works by a completely different mechanism — depleting a nutrient from the blood.

overallFeedback.feedback: L-asparaginase breaks down asparagine in the blood. ALL cancer cells cannot make their own asparagine — they depend entirely on the blood supply. Depleting it starves the cancer cells while normal cells, which can synthesise asparagine, are unaffected.
L-asparaginase exploits a metabolic vulnerability: ALL cells cannot synthesise their own asparagine, so removing it from the blood selectively starves them while normal cells survive.

interactions.label: A jaundiced patient has markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase and elevated gamm

params.question: A jaundiced patient has markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase and elevated gamma-glutamyl transferase, with only mildly raised transaminases. This pattern most likely indicates:

answers.text: Acute viral hepatitis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: In acute viral hepatitis, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is markedly elevated with an AST/ALT ratio below one. This patient's dominant finding — high ALP with elevated GGT and mild transaminases — points to obstruction, not hepatocellular damage.

answers.text: Biliary obstruction

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase combined with elevated gamma-glutamyl transferase confirms a biliary origin. The raised GGT confirms the ALP is coming from bile duct cells rather than bone — this is the classic pattern for biliary obstruction.

answers.text: Alcoholic liver disease

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Alcoholic liver disease classically shows an AST/ALT (De Ritis) ratio above 2 with elevated GGT. Here, the dominant finding is markedly elevated ALP, pointing to biliary obstruction rather than alcohol-related hepatocellular damage.

answers.text: Acute pancreatitis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Acute pancreatitis is diagnosed by markedly elevated lipase (often ten times normal) and elevated amylase. Alkaline phosphatase and GGT are not primary markers of pancreatic inflammation.

overallFeedback.feedback: The pattern matters more than any single value. High ALP + elevated GGT = biliary obstruction. High ALT with AST/ALT < 1 = viral hepatitis. AST/ALT > 2 with elevated GGT = alcoholic liver disease. Lipase ten times normal = acute pancreatitis.
When ALP is markedly elevated and GGT confirms a biliary source, think biliary obstruction — such as a gallstone blocking the bile duct. GGT rules out bone as the source of elevated ALP.
